mnemonics.title: CLOUD Challenges
mnemonics.content:
C - Cooling energy (30-40%)
L - Low utilization (15-25%)
O - Overhead (nested virtualization)
U - Underutilization waste
D - Dark silicon problem


C - Carbon footprint (geographic)
H - Heat management
A - Accountability (multi-tenancy)
L - Lack of standards
L - Lock-in (carbon)
E - E-waste generation
N - Network transfer energy
G - Greenwashing concerns
E - Energy transparency missing
S - Sprawl (VM proliferation)

mnemonics.title: Green Cloud 5 Major Challenges
mnemonics.content:
Remember as 'D.E.S.T.I.N':
D - Data center energy (cooling)
E - Energy inefficiency (low utilization)
S - Standardization gap
T - Transparency deficit
I - Indirect impacts (rebound effect)
N - Network carbon cost

acronyms.usage: Measures data center efficiency. Ideal = 1.0, Typical = 1.1-2.0

acronyms.expansion: Power Usage Effectiveness = Total Facility Energy / IT Equipment Energy
acronyms.usage: Used to evaluate how much energy actually powers computing vs overhead
Software emulation of physical computers; key to cloud virtualization
Inverse of PUE (DCiE = 1/PUE × 100%); expressed as percentage
Company offering cloud services (AWS, Azure, GCP)
Discarded electronic equipment from data centers

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pizza - cooling is 30-40% (large slice), IT equipment is ~40%, and infrastructure losses are 20-30%. Only the IT portion does useful work.
Picture 4 empty seats in a 16-seat bus running - still burning fuel. Servers at 20% utilization waste 60-70% of energy even when 'empty'.
Think of PUE as a funnel - wider top (total energy) narrowing to IT energy at bottom. Lower ratio = less wasted energy 'on the sides'.
Like getting a fuel-efficient car then driving more miles - the savings get offset by increased usage.

analogies.analogy: Like an apartment building - all residents share common utilities (elevator, water heater), making it hard to measure each unit's exact consumption.
Like having too many browser tabs open - each consumes memory/energy even when not actively used.
Leaving all lights on in a building with just one person working - wastes energy but seems convenient.
Buying a water-efficient washing machine and then doing laundry more often - using more water than before.
Like miles-per-gallon for cars - lower numbers mean more efficiency, but different 'vehicles' (data centers) have very different ratings.
Having a 10-story building but only being able to power 3 floors at a time due to electrical limits - wasted capacity.
